segments.narrationText: Hello, I'm Kwen TTS, and I'll be narrating this presentation for EvYatar.
Today we're looking at MCP SEVAL — an MCP server with sixteen evaluation tools that give your AI agent full access to the Sydney evaluation platform.

You've shipped a change to improve search grounding in Copilot. But did it actually work? To find out, you'd open the SEVAL dashboard, create a job, wait for it to finish, then manually sift through scorecards and metrics across multiple tabs.

Hundreds of utterances. Multiple metrics per utterance — quality, grounding, citations, safety, checklists. Control versus experiment comparisons. P-values and statistical significance. All spread across different dashboard views with no single place to get the full picture.

That's the problem MCP SEVAL solves. Instead of clicking through dashboards, your agent creates jobs, polls for completion, reads scorecards, and drills into per-utterance metrics — all in one conversation.
Sixteen tools across four categories. The full SEVAL lifecycle — from job creation to per-utterance diagnosis — now accessible to your AI agent.

{#conn}Connectivity — two tools for health checks and template discovery. {#lifecycle}Job Lifecycle — six tools: list, get, create, cancel, rerun, rename. {#results}Results and Metrics — seven tools for scorecards, utterance queries, conversation detail, and file downloads. {#templates}And Templates — version history and build selection.
The power is in how these tools chain together. Create a job, pull the scorecard, find regressions, drill into the utterance, get a Dev-UI debug link — all in one conversation.

Let's start with creating a job. The agent calls 'list job templates' to find available templates, then 'get template versions' to pick a stable build. This gives you the configuration structure to work with.

Next, the agent calls 'create job' with the template, version, and your configuration — control and experiment flights, dataset, metrics, scraping options. The job is submitted and a numeric job ID is returned.
Template to running job in one conversation. The agent handles template lookup, version selection, and configuration — you just describe what you want to evaluate.
Once a job completes, the agent calls 'get job metrics' to pull the full scorecard. It returns control values, experiment values, deltas, sample sizes, and p-values for every metric.
The agent interprets the results: SbS Leo v3 improved by two points, statistically significant at p equals zero point zero three. Ground Leo dropped by one point five, also significant.

This is the quality-versus-grounding tradeoff — a well-known pattern in SEVAL analysis. Improving answer quality can reduce grounding fidelity. The agent recognizes this tradeoff and explains it, so you don't need to cross-reference columns manually.
Scorecard to actionable insight in seconds. The agent reads the metrics, spots the tradeoff, and tells you exactly where to look next.

The scorecard showed a grounding regression. Let's find out why. The agent uses 'query utterance metrics' to find which specific utterances regressed on Ground Leo. It returns per-utterance scores sorted by severity.
For the worst utterance, it calls 'query utterance detail' — returning the full query text, control and experiment scores, and all metric breakdowns.

Here's the key: the agent calls 'query utterance conversation' to get a Dev-UI side-by-side debug link. Click it and you see control versus experiment for that exact utterance. Or pass the conversation ID to the Dev-UI MCP for programmatic debugging.
From scorecard regression to root cause investigation — the agent finds the worst utterance, surfaces the detail, and gives you the tools to act on it.

The agent can download output files using 'get job output' and 'download job file'. For MChat jobs, it automatically falls back to the Heron download path — you don't need to know which blob backend stores the data.
Lifecycle management is just as easy. 'Rerun job' to retry with the same config. 'Cancel job' to stop a stuck run. 'Rename job' to label your experiments for tracking.
The full lifecycle — create, monitor, analyze, download, rerun — accessible through natural conversation.

What if SEVAL finds a regression and your agent can debug it automatically? Add both SEVAL MCP and Dev-UI MCP to the same workspace — the agent gets evaluation metrics and conversation debugging in one context.
SEVAL MCP identifies the worst utterance and extracts the conversation ID. Dev-UI MCP loads that conversation, pulls the symptom report, and diagnoses the root cause. All in one conversation.
Two MCP servers, one conversation. From regression detection to root cause diagnosis without switching tools.
This is the vision: a closed-loop quality workflow. Run a SEVAL job, find regressions, debug them, fix the code, rerun to verify.
Evaluate, diagnose, fix, verify. Each step delegated to the agent — you describe the goal.
MCP SEVAL. Not just metrics — a quality engineer.
Getting started takes two minutes. Step one: register the marketplace. Run slash plugin marketplace add gim-home slash ai-developer-toolkit.
Step two: install the SEVAL plugin. Run slash plugin install seval-tools at ai-developer-toolkit. All sixteen tools are immediately available.
That's it. Ask your agent to create a SEVAL job, analyze results, or diagnose regressions.
Remember that wall of scorecard metrics? The manual spreadsheet parsing, the p-value cross-referencing — {#dismiss}that's behind you now.
Your agent creates evaluation jobs, reads scorecards, diagnoses regressions per-utterance, and bridges to Dev-UI for root cause analysis.
MCP SEVAL. Not just metrics — a quality engineer.
